nodes.label: Middle East
nodes.summary: The cradle of Abrahamic religions — Judaism, Christianity, and Islam all emerged here.

nodes.label: South Asia
nodes.summary: Birthplace of Dharmic religions — Hinduism, Buddhism, Jainism, and Sikhism.

nodes.label: East Asia
nodes.summary: Home to Confucianism, Taoism, Shinto, and unique Buddhist traditions.

nodes.label: Europe
nodes.summary: Where Christianity split, reformed, and diversified into hundreds of denominations.

nodes.label: Africa
nodes.summary: Ancient traditional religions and rich syncretic traditions born from the diaspora.

nodes.label: Americas
nodes.summary: New World religious movements — from Pentecostalism to Mormonism, Santeria to Candomble.

nodes.label: Global / Cross-Regional
nodes.summary: Ancient religions, new religious movements, and traditions that transcend geographic boundaries.

nodes.label: Iran / Persia
nodes.summary: Iranian religions — Zoroastrianism, Manichaeism, and Yazidism shaped monotheism and dualism worldwide.

nodes.label: Zoroastrianism
nodes.summary: Founded by Zoroaster ~1500-500 BCE in Persia. First major monotheistic religion — introduced heaven, hell, angels, final judgment, and cosmic dualism between good (Ahura Mazda) and evil (Angra Mainyu).

nodes.label: Manichaeism
nodes.summary: Founded by Mani in 3rd century CE Persia. Syncretic religion blending Zoroastrian dualism, Christian theology, and Buddhist cosmology. Once spread from Rome to China.

nodes.label: Yazidism
nodes.summary: Ancient Kurdish religion centered on Melek Taus (Peacock Angel). Blends elements of Zoroastrianism, Islam, and local traditions. Primarily in Kurdistan, ~12th century formalization.

nodes.label: Judaism
nodes.summary: The oldest Abrahamic religion, ~2000 BCE in Canaan. Covenant between God and Abraham's descendants. Torah as foundational text. Monotheism, ethical law, and peoplehood.

nodes.label: Orthodox Judaism
nodes.summary: Traditional Judaism maintaining halakha (Jewish law) as divinely authored and binding. Includes Modern Orthodox and Haredi (ultra-Orthodox) streams.

nodes.label: Hasidic Judaism
nodes.summary: Founded by Baal Shem Tov in 1740s Ukraine. Mystical, joyful approach to God through prayer, song, and devotion. Organized into dynastic courts (Chabad, Satmar, Breslov, etc.).

nodes.label: Conservative Judaism
nodes.summary: Middle path between Orthodox and Reform. Maintains halakha as binding but subject to historical evolution. Founded 1880s in Germany/USA by Zacharias Frankel.

nodes.label: Reform Judaism
nodes.summary: Founded 1810s in Germany. Torah as God-inspired but not literal divine dictation. Halakha is non-binding; emphasis on ethical monotheism and social justice.

nodes.label: Reconstructionist Judaism
nodes.summary: Founded by Mordecai Kaplan in 1920s USA. Judaism as an evolving civilization rather than a religion. God as process, not person.

nodes.label: Karaite Judaism
nodes.summary: 8th century CE, Mesopotamia. Rejects the Oral Torah (Talmud), relying solely on the written Hebrew Bible. Pre-dates the Orthodox/Reform split by a millennium.

nodes.label: Samaritanism
nodes.summary: Ancient Israelite religion, ~6th century BCE. Accepts only the Torah (Pentateuch), worships on Mount Gerizim. Fewer than 1000 adherents remain today.

nodes.label: Christianity
nodes.summary: Founded ~33 CE in Roman Judea by followers of Jesus of Nazareth. World's largest religion (~2.4 billion). Central beliefs: incarnation, crucifixion, resurrection, and salvation through grace.

nodes.label: Church of the East
nodes.summary: Split after Council of Ephesus (431 CE). Nestorian theology — Christ has two natures and two persons. Spread along Silk Road to China and India. Also called Assyrian Church.

nodes.label: Oriental Orthodoxy
nodes.summary: Split after Council of Chalcedon (451 CE) over Christ's nature. Miaphysite theology — one united divine-human nature. Includes Coptic, Armenian, Ethiopian, and Syriac churches.

nodes.label: Coptic Orthodox
nodes.summary: Egyptian Christianity tracing to Mark the Evangelist (~42 CE). Largest Christian community in the Middle East. Rich monastic tradition — Anthony the Great founded Christian monasticism here.

nodes.label: Armenian Apostolic
nodes.summary: Armenia became the first Christian nation in 301 CE under King Tiridates III. Apostolic tradition from Bartholomew and Thaddeus. Unique liturgy and alphabet created for Scripture.

nodes.label: Ethiopian Orthodox Tewahedo
nodes.summary: One of the oldest Christian churches, 4th century CE. Unique canon including Book of Enoch. Claims the Ark of the Covenant. Deeply intertwined with Ethiopian national identity.

nodes.label: Eastern Orthodoxy
nodes.summary: Split from Rome in the Great Schism of 1054 CE. Disputes over papal authority, the Filioque clause, and liturgical practices. Organized as autocephalous national churches.

nodes.label: Greek Orthodox
nodes.summary: The Ecumenical Patriarchate of Constantinople — 'first among equals' in Eastern Orthodoxy. Greek theological and liturgical tradition, Byzantine chant, and iconography.

nodes.label: Russian Orthodox
nodes.summary: Christianity adopted by Prince Vladimir of Kyiv in 988 CE. Largest Eastern Orthodox church. Deep connection to Russian national identity, art, and literature.

nodes.label: Roman Catholicism
nodes.summary: World's largest Christian church (~1.4 billion). Led by the Pope in Rome. Claims apostolic succession from Peter. Defined by sacramental theology, papal authority, and Sacred Tradition alongside Scripture.

nodes.label: Old Catholic Church
nodes.summary: Split from Rome in 1870 after rejecting papal infallibility (First Vatican Council). Maintains Catholic liturgy and sacraments but rejects papal supremacy.

nodes.label: Protestantism
nodes.summary: Born from Martin Luther's 95 Theses (1517). Core principles: sola scriptura (Scripture alone), sola fide (faith alone), priesthood of all believers. Fragmented into thousands of denominations.

nodes.label: Lutheranism
nodes.summary: Founded by Martin Luther in 1517, Germany. Justification by faith alone. Retained liturgical worship and sacraments while rejecting papal authority. ~80 million adherents.

nodes.label: Reformed / Calvinist
nodes.summary: Founded by Zwingli and Calvin in 1520s Switzerland. Predestination, sovereignty of God, TULIP theology. Influenced Puritans, Huguenots, and Presbyterian traditions.

nodes.label: Anglicanism
nodes.summary: Established 1534 by Henry VIII's break with Rome. Via media (middle way) between Catholicism and Protestantism. Book of Common Prayer. ~85 million in the Anglican Communion.

nodes.label: Anabaptism
nodes.summary: Radical Reformation, 1525 Switzerland. Adult baptism, pacifism, separation of church and state. Persecuted by both Catholics and Protestants. Parent of Mennonites, Amish, Hutterites.

nodes.label: Mennonites
nodes.summary: Founded by Menno Simons in 1536, Netherlands. Pacifist, community-oriented, simple living. Range from traditional to progressive congregations worldwide.

nodes.label: Amish
nodes.summary: Split from Mennonites in 1693, led by Jakob Ammann. Known for plain dress, horse-and-buggy, and Ordnung (community rules). Reject most modern technology.

nodes.label: Baptist
nodes.summary: Founded 1609 in Amsterdam by John Smyth. Believer's baptism by immersion, congregational governance, religious liberty. ~100 million worldwide; Southern Baptist Convention is the largest Protestant body in the USA.

nodes.label: Methodism
nodes.summary: Founded by John Wesley in 1730s England. Emphasis on personal holiness, social justice, and methodical devotion. Gave rise to the Holiness movement and influenced Pentecostalism.

nodes.label: Quakers (Society of Friends)
nodes.summary: Founded by George Fox in 1650s England. Inner Light — direct experience of God without clergy, sacraments, or creeds. Pioneered abolition, women's rights, and pacifism.

nodes.label: Presbyterianism
nodes.summary: Founded by John Knox in 1560s Scotland, rooted in Calvinist theology. Governed by elders (presbyters) rather than bishops. Strong emphasis on education and intellectual rigor.

nodes.label: Pentecostalism
nodes.summary: Born from the Azusa Street Revival (1906, Los Angeles). Emphasis on baptism of the Holy Spirit, speaking in tongues, faith healing, and direct spiritual experience. ~600 million globally — fastest-growing Christian movement.

nodes.label: Adventism
nodes.summary: Emerged from the Millerite movement in 1860s USA after the Great Disappointment (1844). Seventh-day Adventists emphasize Saturday Sabbath, health reform, and Christ's imminent return.

nodes.label: Mormonism (LDS)
nodes.summary: Founded by Joseph Smith in 1830, New York. Book of Mormon as additional scripture. Latter-day Saints emphasize continuing revelation, temple ordinances, and family. ~17 million members.

nodes.label: Jehovah's Witnesses
nodes.summary: Founded by Charles Taze Russell in 1870s USA. Non-trinitarian, reject blood transfusions, refuse military service. Door-to-door evangelism. ~8.7 million active members.

nodes.label: Islam
nodes.summary: Founded by Prophet Muhammad in 622 CE, Arabia. Submission to the will of God (Allah). Five Pillars: shahada, prayer, charity, fasting, pilgrimage. ~1.9 billion adherents worldwide.

nodes.label: Sunni Islam
nodes.summary: The majority branch (~85-90% of Muslims). Follows the Sunnah of Muhammad and accepts Abu Bakr as first rightful caliph. Four major schools of jurisprudence (madhabs).

nodes.label: Hanafi School
nodes.summary: Founded by Abu Hanifa in 8th century Iraq. Most liberal of the four madhabs, emphasizing reason (ra'y) and analogy (qiyas). Dominant in Turkey, South Asia, Central Asia.

nodes.label: Maliki School
nodes.summary: Founded by Malik ibn Anas in 8th century Medina. Emphasizes the practice of the people of Medina as a source of law. Dominant in North and West Africa.

nodes.label: Shafi'i School
nodes.summary: Founded by al-Shafi'i in 9th century Egypt. Systematized Islamic jurisprudence (usul al-fiqh). Dominant in East Africa, Southeast Asia, and Yemen.

nodes.label: Hanbali School
nodes.summary: Founded by Ahmad ibn Hanbal in 9th century Baghdad. Most conservative madhab — strict adherence to Quran and hadith, minimal use of reason. Dominant in Saudi Arabia.

nodes.label: Salafism / Wahhabism
nodes.summary: Founded by Muhammad ibn Abd al-Wahhab in 18th century Arabia. Return to the practices of the salaf (early Muslims). Puritanical, anti-innovation (bid'ah). Alliance with House of Saud.

nodes.label: Shia Islam
nodes.summary: ~10-15% of Muslims. Believe Ali ibn Abi Talib was Muhammad's rightful successor. Emphasis on the Imamate — divinely guided leaders from Muhammad's family (Ahl al-Bayt).

nodes.label: Twelver Shia
nodes.summary: Largest Shia branch. Follows twelve Imams descended from Ali, the twelfth (Mahdi) in occultation since 874 CE. Dominant in Iran, Iraq, Lebanon, Bahrain.

nodes.label: Ismaili Shia
nodes.summary: Split from Twelvers over the seventh Imam. Led by the Aga Khan. Emphasize esoteric (batini) interpretation of the Quran. Nizari branch is the largest today.

nodes.label: Zaydi Shia
nodes.summary: Closest Shia branch to Sunni Islam. Named after Zayd ibn Ali. Reject divine right of Imams — any descendant of Ali can lead if qualified. Dominant in Yemen.

nodes.label: Alawites
nodes.summary: Syncretic sect from 9th century Syria. Deify Ali, incorporate Christian and pre-Islamic elements. Secretive theology. Ruling elite of modern Syria (Assad family).

nodes.label: Sufism
nodes.summary: Islamic mysticism, 8th-9th century. Direct experience of God through dhikr (remembrance), poetry, music, and ecstatic practice. Cross-cuts Sunni and Shia. Rumi, Al-Ghazali, Ibn Arabi.

nodes.label: Ibadi Islam
nodes.summary: 7th century, Oman. Pre-dates the Sunni-Shia split — neither Sunni nor Shia. Moderate, emphasizes community consensus. Dominant in Oman, pockets in North Africa.

nodes.label: Ahmadiyya
nodes.summary: Founded by Mirza Ghulam Ahmad in 1889, Punjab. Claims to be the promised Messiah and Mahdi. Emphasizes peaceful propagation. Persecuted as heretical by mainstream Islam.

nodes.label: Baha'i Faith
nodes.summary: Founded by Baha'u'llah in 1863, Persia. Emerged from Shia Islam via the Bab movement. Progressive revelation — all major religions are stages of one unfolding truth. Unity of humanity.

nodes.label: Druze
nodes.summary: Founded 1017 CE in Fatimid Egypt. Esoteric offshoot of Ismaili Islam blending Neoplatonism, Gnosticism, and Zoroastrian elements. Closed community — no conversion in or out. ~1 million in Lebanon, Syria, Israel.

nodes.label: Mandaeism
nodes.summary: Ancient Gnostic religion, 1st-2nd century CE Mesopotamia. Reveres John the Baptist, rejects Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad. Ritual water baptism (masbuta) central to worship.

nodes.label: Hinduism
nodes.summary: World's oldest living religion, ~1500 BCE in the Indus Valley. No single founder — a vast family of traditions united by concepts of dharma, karma, samsara, and moksha. ~1.2 billion adherents.

nodes.label: Vaishnavism
nodes.summary: Worship of Vishnu and his avatars (Rama, Krishna). Largest Hindu denomination. Bhakti (devotion) as the primary path to God. Includes ISKCON (Hare Krishna).

nodes.label: Shaivism
nodes.summary: Worship of Shiva as the supreme deity. Rich philosophical tradition including Kashmir Shaivism (non-dual). Emphasizes yoga, meditation, and asceticism.

nodes.label: Shaktism
nodes.summary: Worship of Shakti — the divine feminine, manifested as Durga, Kali, Parvati, Lakshmi. Tantric practices. Particularly strong in Bengal and Assam.

nodes.label: Smartism
nodes.summary: Founded by Adi Shankara (~8th century CE). Advaita Vedanta — non-dualism, all deities are manifestations of one Brahman. Worships five deities equally (Panchayatana).

nodes.label: Vedanta
nodes.summary: The culmination of Vedic philosophy (Upanishads). Three major schools: Advaita (non-dual), Vishishtadvaita (qualified non-dual), Dvaita (dual). Most influential Hindu philosophical tradition.

nodes.label: Yoga (Philosophical School)
nodes.summary: One of six orthodox Hindu schools. Patanjali's Yoga Sutras (~2nd century BCE) — eight-limbed path to liberation through ethical conduct, posture, breath control, and meditation.

nodes.label: Buddhism
nodes.summary: Founded by Siddhartha Gautama (the Buddha) ~5th century BCE in India. Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path to end suffering (dukkha). ~500 million adherents worldwide.

nodes.label: Theravada Buddhism
nodes.summary: The 'School of the Elders' — oldest surviving Buddhist school, ~3rd century BCE. Pali Canon as authoritative text. Emphasis on individual enlightenment (arahant). Dominant in Sri Lanka, Thailand, Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos.

nodes.label: Mahayana Buddhism
nodes.summary: The 'Great Vehicle' — emerged ~1st century CE. Bodhisattva ideal: delay your own enlightenment to help all sentient beings. Expanded canon with new sutras. Dominant in East Asia.

nodes.label: Pure Land Buddhism
nodes.summary: Devotion to Amitabha Buddha for rebirth in the Western Pure Land. Most popular Mahayana school in East Asia. Salvation through faith and recitation of Amitabha's name (nembutsu/nianfo).

nodes.label: Zen / Chan Buddhism
nodes.summary: Originated as Chan in 6th century China, transmitted to Japan as Zen. Direct pointing to the mind, seeing one's true nature. Zazen (sitting meditation), koans, and minimalist aesthetics.

nodes.label: Nichiren Buddhism
nodes.summary: Founded by Nichiren in 1253, Japan. Devotion to the Lotus Sutra as the supreme teaching. Chanting Nam-myoho-renge-kyo. Includes modern Soka Gakkai International (~12 million members).

nodes.label: Tendai / Tiantai
nodes.summary: Founded by Zhiyi in 6th century China, brought to Japan by Saicho in 805. Lotus Sutra as supreme. Comprehensive system integrating all Buddhist teachings. Mother of Japanese Buddhism — Zen, Pure Land, and Nichiren all emerged from Tendai.

nodes.label: Vajrayana Buddhism
nodes.summary: The 'Diamond Vehicle' — esoteric Buddhism emphasizing tantric practices, mantras, mudras, mandalas, and guru-disciple transmission. Emerged ~7th century CE in India, flourished in Tibet and Mongolia.

nodes.label: Gelug School
nodes.summary: Founded by Tsongkhapa in 1409, Tibet. 'Yellow Hat' school — emphasis on monastic discipline, logic, and graduated path (lamrim). The Dalai Lama tradition belongs to this school.

nodes.label: Kagyu School
nodes.summary: Founded 11th century, Tibet. 'Oral Lineage' — emphasis on meditation practice and direct guru transmission. Milarepa the famous yogi. Karmapa as head lama.

nodes.label: Nyingma School
nodes.summary: Oldest Tibetan Buddhist school, 8th century. Founded by Padmasambhava (Guru Rinpoche). Dzogchen (Great Perfection) as the highest teaching. 'Red Hat' tradition.

nodes.label: Jainism
nodes.summary: Founded by Mahavira ~6th century BCE in India. Radical non-violence (ahimsa), asceticism, and karma as material substance. Two main sects: Digambara (sky-clad) and Svetambara (white-clad). ~4-5 million adherents.

nodes.label: Sikhism
nodes.summary: Founded by Guru Nanak in 1499, Punjab. Monotheism, equality, service, and honest living. Synthesizes Hindu devotionalism and Islamic monotheism while rejecting caste and idol worship. ~30 million adherents.

nodes.label: Confucianism
nodes.summary: Founded by Confucius ~500 BCE, China. Ethical philosophy emphasizing ren (benevolence), li (ritual propriety), filial piety, and social harmony. Shaped East Asian civilization for 2500 years.

nodes.label: Neo-Confucianism
nodes.summary: Revival movement from 11th century Song Dynasty. Zhu Xi synthesized Confucian ethics with Buddhist and Taoist metaphysics. Li (principle) and qi (vital force) as cosmic framework. Dominated Chinese thought for 700 years.

nodes.label: Taoism
nodes.summary: Philosophical and religious tradition, ~4th century BCE China. The Tao (Way) that cannot be named. Wu wei (non-action), yin-yang, naturalness. Laozi's Tao Te Ching and Zhuangzi's parables.

nodes.label: Shinto
nodes.summary: Indigenous religion of Japan, prehistoric origins. Kami (spirits/gods) inhabit nature, ancestors, and sacred places. Ritual purity, shrine worship, and seasonal festivals. No founder or central scripture.

nodes.label: Chinese Folk Religion
nodes.summary: Syncretic blend of Confucian ethics, Taoist cosmology, Buddhist karmic beliefs, and ancestor worship. Practiced by hundreds of millions. No formal organization — embedded in daily life and festivals.

nodes.label: Cao Dai
nodes.summary: Founded 1926, Vietnam. Explicitly syncretic — unifies Buddhism, Taoism, Confucianism, Christianity, and Spiritism. Saints include Victor Hugo, Sun Yat-sen, and Joan of Arc. ~5 million adherents.

nodes.label: Korean Shamanism (Muism)
nodes.summary: Prehistoric Korean indigenous religion. Female shamans (mudang) mediate between human and spirit worlds through ecstatic rituals (gut). Persists alongside Buddhism and Christianity.

nodes.label: Yoruba Religion
nodes.summary: Ancient West African religion (Nigeria, Benin). Supreme deity Olodumare and pantheon of orishas (Shango, Oshun, Ogun, Yemoja). Ifa divination system. Ancestor of diaspora religions.

nodes.label: West African Vodun
nodes.summary: Ancient religion of the Fon and Ewe peoples (Benin, Togo). Spirits (loa/vodun) mediate between humans and the supreme creator. Rituals, possession, herbalism. Source tradition for diaspora Vodou.

nodes.label: Akan Religion
nodes.summary: Traditional religion of the Akan people (Ghana, Ivory Coast). Supreme God (Nyame), lesser deities (abosom), ancestor spirits. Adinkra symbols encode philosophical concepts.

nodes.label: Santeria (Regla de Ocha)
nodes.summary: Afro-Cuban syncretic religion, 16th-19th century. Yoruba orishas mapped to Catholic saints by enslaved Africans. Animal sacrifice, divination, drumming. ~100 million practitioners across the Americas.

nodes.label: Candomble
nodes.summary: Afro-Brazilian religion, 16th-19th century. Yoruba, Fon, and Bantu traditions blended with Catholicism. Orixas (spirits), terreiros (temples), and ecstatic dance ceremonies.

nodes.label: Haitian Vodou
nodes.summary: Syncretic religion combining West African Vodun with Catholicism, born in Haiti during slavery. Lwa (spirits), possession rituals, and Veve symbols. Deeply tied to Haitian identity and revolution.

nodes.label: Rastafari
nodes.summary: Emerged in 1930s Jamaica. Haile Selassie I of Ethiopia as God incarnate (Jah). African repatriation, rejection of Babylon (Western society), cannabis as sacrament. Reggae as spiritual expression.

nodes.label: Gnosticism
nodes.summary: Diverse set of ancient religions (1st-3rd century CE). Salvation through gnosis (secret knowledge). Material world created by a flawed demiurge. Nag Hammadi library rediscovered in 1945.

nodes.label: Wicca
nodes.summary: Founded by Gerald Gardner in 1940s England. Modern pagan witchcraft honoring the Goddess and the Horned God. Wheel of the Year, ritual magic, and the Wiccan Rede ('harm none').

nodes.label: Scientology
nodes.summary: Founded by L. Ron Hubbard in 1954, USA. Spiritual technology (Dianetics) to clear engrams and reach the state of 'Clear'. Controversial — classified as religion in some countries, cult in others.

nodes.label: Unitarian Universalism
nodes.summary: Merger of Unitarianism and Universalism in 1961, USA. No creed — draws from all world religions, humanism, and reason. Seven Principles emphasizing inherent worth and dignity.

nodes.label: Spiritism
nodes.summary: Codified by Allan Kardec in 1857, France. Communication with spirits of the dead, reincarnation, moral evolution. ~15 million practitioners, primarily in Brazil.

nodes.label: Tenrikyo
nodes.summary: Founded by Nakayama Miki in 1838, Japan. Joyous life through returning to the mind of God the Parent. One of Japan's largest new religious movements (~2 million adherents).

nodes.label: Ancient Egyptian Religion
nodes.summary: ~3000 BCE to 4th century CE. Polytheistic — Ra, Osiris, Isis, Horus, Anubis. Afterlife central — Book of the Dead, mummification, judgment by Maat. One of the oldest documented religions.

nodes.label: Ancient Greek Religion
nodes.summary: ~800 BCE to 4th century CE. Olympian gods (Zeus, Athena, Apollo), mystery cults (Eleusinian, Dionysian), oracles, and philosophical theology. Profoundly influenced Christianity and Western thought.

edges.label: Shared Israelite roots — diverged over which temple and which scriptures
Christianity emerged from Second Temple Judaism as a messianic movement
Split after Council of Ephesus 431 CE — Nestorian Christology
Split after Council of Chalcedon 451 CE — Miaphysite Christology
Great Schism of 1054 — split from Rome over Filioque and papal authority
Claims continuity from Peter — Great Schism of 1054 formalized the split
Rejected papal infallibility at Vatican I, 1870
Protestant Reformation 1517 — broke from Rome over justification and authority
Political break from Rome 1534, theological reformation followed gradually
Radical Reformation — went further than Luther and Calvin
Split from Mennonites in 1693 over stricter discipline (Meidung)
John Wesley began as Anglican revival, became separate denomination
John Knox brought Calvinist theology to Scotland with presbyterian governance
Grew from Holiness movement rooted in Methodism, Azusa Street Revival 1906
Millerite movement after Great Disappointment of 1844
Restorationist — claims to restore original Christianity via new revelation
Restorationist — rejects Trinity, emphasizes eschatology
Islam acknowledges Abraham, Moses, and Jewish prophets; shares monotheism and dietary laws
Islam reveres Jesus (Isa) as a prophet; Mary (Maryam) has her own surah
Puritanical movement rooted in Hanbali jurisprudence
Split over the seventh Imam — followed Ismail instead of Musa
Highly syncretic — deifies Ali, incorporates pre-Islamic elements
Mystical dimension — crosses Sunni/Shia boundaries
Pre-dates the Sunni-Shia split — neither Sunni nor Shia
Claims messianic fulfillment within Islam — considered heretical by mainstream
Emerged from Shia Islam via the Bab movement in 19th century Persia
Originated as an Ismaili sect, then diverged radically
Incorporates Neoplatonic and Gnostic elements
Buddha born Hindu — adopted karma, samsara, meditation but rejected caste, Vedic authority, and atman
Contemporary with Buddhism — shares karma and moksha concepts but radicalizes ahimsa
Guru Nanak drew from Hindu Bhakti devotional tradition
Guru Nanak drew from Sufi mystical monotheism and egalitarianism
Absorbed Buddhist metaphysics while reasserting Confucian ethics
Chan/Zen is a Buddhist-Taoist synthesis — naturalness, spontaneity, and wordless transmission
Confucian ethics embedded in folk practice — filial piety, ancestor worship
Taoist cosmology, deities, and practices woven into folk religion
Buddhist karma and bodhisattvas absorbed into Chinese folk practice
After Babylonian Exile, Zoroastrian concepts of heaven/hell, angels, resurrection, and cosmic dualism entered Judaism
Mani synthesized Zoroastrian dualism with Christian and Buddhist elements
Incorporates Zoroastrian dualistic concepts and reverence for fire/sun
Sheikh Adi ibn Musafir, a Sufi mystic, formalized Yazidi theology
Yoruba orishas mapped to Catholic saints by enslaved Africans in Cuba
Catholic saints and liturgical calendar syncretized with Yoruba practice
Brazilian continuation of Yoruba religion, blended with Catholic elements
West African Vodun transplanted to Haiti and syncretized with Catholicism
Draws from the Bible, especially Old Testament and Revelation
Ethiopian Christianity and the Solomonic dynasty inform Rastafari theology
Early Christian Gnostic sects — provoked orthodoxy to define itself in opposition
Gnostic cosmology of trapped light-sparks directly influenced Mani
Greek Logos concept adopted in Gospel of John; Neoplatonism shaped Augustine and medieval theology
Moses narrative, Exodus, possible monotheistic influence from Akhenaten
Great Schism — Filioque clause, papal supremacy, leavened vs unleavened bread
Sola scriptura vs Scripture+Tradition; sola fide vs faith+works; papal authority vs priesthood of all believers
Succession crisis — Abu Bakr vs Ali; nature of the Imamate; different hadith collections
Arahantship vs bodhisattva ideal; Pali Canon vs expanded sutras; individual vs universal liberation
Torah as divine vs God-inspired; halakha binding vs non-binding; tradition fixed vs evolving
Luther's 95 Theses (1517) launched the Reformation
Radical Reformation — went beyond Luther's reforms
Holiness movement from Methodism directly birthed Pentecostalism at Azusa Street
Rejected caste system, Vedic authority, atman (soul), and creator God
Contemporary movements — both rejected Vedic authority, share karma/moksha but differ on self and asceticism
